22.1.label: Question 22.1
22.1.scenario:
A health insurance company sells accelerated CI insurance contracts with term of three years.
Premiums are payable annually in advance. The sum insured of €50,000 is payable at the end of
the year that a claim occurs.

The pricing basis uses the following best estimate assumptions:
Claim rate:                               0.1% per annum
Interest:                                 3% per annum
Expenses:            initial:             €70 incurred at outset
renewal:             €5 incurred when second and third premiums are received
claim:               €80 incurred when the benefit is paid
Profit loading:                           €30

Note that the cost of capital associated with the solvency requirements has been ignored in the
pricing calculation for simplicity.

(i).question:
Show that the premium for this policy is €86.26.                                                [3]

The regulator believes that the following assumptions would represent a prudent estimate of
future experience:
Claim rate:                               0.13% per annum
Interest:                                 2% per annum
Expenses:            renewal:             €6 incurred when second and third premiums are received
claim:               €90 incurred when the benefit is paid

The regulator is prepared to allow companies to calculate reserves on a best estimate basis if they
hold additional solvency capital calculated as 0.091% of the sum at risk.

Premium: The premium P is calculated by equating the expected present value of the premiums with the
expected present value of the claims, expenses and profit loading.
## P  86.26: Note that the question has made no reference to lapses, so they have been ignored here (although
in practice, an allowance for lapses would be made).
The premium of €86.26 has just been received and the expenses of €70 have just been paid
(assuming that the actual expense experience was the same as the best estimate). So the assets
Note that there would often be a negative asset share at the outset of the policy.
## V  13.74: Note that the reserve is negative as is often the case when calculating a best estimate reserve for
V  32.02: Note that the reserve is positive as is usually the case when calculating a prudent reserve.
(ii)(d)   Supervisory reserve (best estimate basis) plus solvency capital
SCR  45.51: So the total solvency requirement is €31.77 (45.51  13.74).                                                          [1]

(ii).question:
The contract has just been sold, the premium has been paid and the initial expenses have
been incurred. Calculate:
(a)        the change in the assets
(b)        the supervisory reserves calculated on the best estimate basis used in pricing
(assuming there is no solvency capital requirement)
(c)        the supervisory reserves calculated on the regulator’s prudent basis (assuming
there is no solvency capital requirement)
(d)        the sum of the supervisory reserves calculated on the best estimate basis and the
solvency capital requirement.

Solution: (i)       Premium
The premium P is calculated by equating the expected present value of the premiums with the
expected present value of the claims, expenses and profit loading.
## P  86.26: Note that the question has made no reference to lapses, so they have been ignored here (although
in practice, an allowance for lapses would be made).
The premium of €86.26 has just been received and the expenses of €70 have just been paid
(assuming that the actual expense experience was the same as the best estimate). So the assets
Note that there would often be a negative asset share at the outset of the policy.
## V  13.74: Note that the reserve is negative as is often the case when calculating a best estimate reserve for
V  32.02: Note that the reserve is positive as is usually the case when calculating a prudent reserve.
(ii)(d)   Supervisory reserve (best estimate basis) plus solvency capital
SCR  45.51: So the total solvency requirement is €31.77 (45.51  13.74).                                                          [1]
(iii)     Comments on results
When the company writes this policy its assets increase by €16.26 and its reserves decrease by
€13.74 if best estimate assumptions are used.                                                  [½]
So the surplus of the company (assets over liabilities) increases by €30 due to writing this policy.
So if reserves are calculated on a best estimate basis (perhaps for accounting or internal
management purposes), a profit of €30 occurs at outset (which is exactly the profit that was
priced for).                                                                                       [½]
However, the situation is quite different if reserves are calculated on a prudent basis. When the
company writes this policy its assets increase by €16.26 and its reserves increase by €32.02.   [½]
So the surplus of the company (assets over liabilities) decreases by €15.76 due to writing this
policy.                                                                                                              [½]
The company now has new business strain, ie the premium of €86.26 is not enough to cover the
initial reserves of €32.02 and the initial expenses of €70.                                [½]
The total solvency requirement is approximately €32 under both the prudent reserving approach
and the best estimate plus solvency capital approach. So both approaches give the same level of
protection to the policyholders (they both require that the company sets aside an extra €45.50
over and above the best estimate cost of paying the liabilities) and have the same new business
strain.                                                                                         [1]
[Maximum 3]
Note that the question was designed so that the two prudent approaches gave the same result.

management purposes), a profit of €30 occurs at outset (which is exactly the profit that was

22.2.label: Question 22.2
22.2.scenario: A health and care insurer is using a market‐consistent approach to set its reserves.

(i).question: State when a risk margin might be applied.

Situation in which a risk margin may be: A risk margin may be applied where it is difficult to obtain market‐consistent assumptions for
… such as morbidity, persistency or expenses.                                                                        [½]
This is because there is not a sufficiently deep and liquid market in which to trade or hedge such
In other words, there is not a market big enough so that large trades would not materially affect
In such cases, a risk margin would be included in respect of such assumptions …                       [½]

(ii).question: State what such a risk margin should reflect.

What a risk margin should reflect: The risk margin should reflect the compensation required by the ‘market’ in return for taking on
those uncertain aspects of the liability cashflows.                                             [1]

(iii).question: State two alternative methods for applying a risk margin.

Two methods for applying a risk margin: 1.        A margin could be added to each assumption for which there is inherent uncertainty. [½]
2.        An overall reserving margin could be determined using the ‘cost of capital’ approach. [½]

(iv).question: Give a formula for an overall risk margin, defining all terms you use.

Formula for an overall risk margin: where: kt is the cost of capital ‘charge’ for time t                                                  [½]
Ct is the required capital at time t                                                       [½]
rt is the risk‐free interest rate for maturity t.                                          [½]

22.3.label: Question 22.3
22.3.question:
Outline the cost of capital approach of determining the risk margin for a market‐consistent
   valuation.

Projection of capital: The cost of capital method involves first projecting forward the future capital that the company is
required to hold at the end of each projection period (eg year) during the run‐off of the business.
The projected capital is the amount required to be held in excess of the projected liabilities at
Calculation of the projected capital might require complex projections, stochastic modelling,
... and should be determined according to the relevant regulatory basis.                             [½]
However, regulations may permit a simplified approach to be used ...                                 [½]
... which might involve selecting a driver which has an approximately linear relationship with the
... so that the projected capital can then be approximated as a percentage of the projected values
Cost of capital charge: These projected capital amounts are then multiplied by a cost of capital rate.                                       [½]
This rate represents the cost of raising incremental capital in excess of the risk‐free rate ...                     [½]
... or alternatively it represents the frictional cost to the company of locking in this capital to earn
a risk‐free rate rather than being able to invest it freely for higher reward.                        [½]
It can be calculated as the excess of the weighted average cost of capital over the risk‐free rate.
It may be dependent on the time period being considered ...                                                          [½]
... or it may be a specified fixed rate (eg 6% per annum in Solvency II).                                            [½]
The overall risk margin is then calculat:          kt is the cost of capital charge for time t                                                                [½]
         Ct is the required capital at time t and                                                                   [½]
         rt is the risk‐free interest rate for maturity t.                                                          [½]

22.4.label: Question 22.4
22.4.scenario:
A health and care insurer sells level immediate needs annuities.

It has the choice of the following assets to invest in:
         a large issue of low grade, short‐term, fixed‐interest corporate bonds, issued by a large
domestic multinational company
         a small issue of high grade, short‐term, fixed‐interest corporate bonds, issued by a small,
private local company
         short‐term, fixed‐interest government bonds.

The corporate bonds provide a similar expected yield, which is significantly higher than the
government bond yield.

(i).question: Which bonds would be most appropriate to back the annuity liabilities?

Appropriate matching bonds: The annuities are level, and all three of the bonds are fixed interest, so they all provide a good
match by nature.                                                                                   [½]
Immediate needs annuities are generally short‐term (typically a few years), …                                        [½]
… so all three of the bonds should be broadly appropriate by term.                                                   [½]
The yield margin between the government and corporate bonds reflects the higher risk of default
… and lower liquidity of the corporate bonds.                                                                        [½]
The government bonds should have the lowest risk of default, …                                                       [½]
… so these may be the preferred option, especially if the insurer is risk‐averse.                                    [½]
The low grade bonds have the highest risk of default, …                                                              [½]
… so these may be deemed inappropriate.                                                                              [½]
The high grade bonds should have a more acceptable level of default risk, …                              [½]
… and the greater return provided by corporate bonds may make these appealing enough to
choose over the government bonds.                                                                        [½]
If the insurer has matched its assets and liabilities, then it would not be expecting to sell the
bonds before maturity, and so liquidity is not an essential feature of the assets.                       [½]
The government bonds should be the most marketable / liquid.                                             [½]
The large issue of low grade company bonds should also be fairly marketable / liquid.                    [½]
However, the small issue of small, private company bonds are likely to be considerably less
marketable / liquid, …                                                                                   [½]
… and so a significant part of their yield will be to compensate for this illiquidity.                   [½]
As liquidity is a feature that is probably not needed by this insurer, these bonds may therefore be
appropriate, …                                                                                   [½]
… as the insurer will be able to benefit from the higher yield without taking on a significant level
of risk.                                                                                           [½]
[Maximum 6]

(ii).question:
How would the choice of bonds affect the discount rate used to value the liabilities in a
market‐consistent valuation?

Solution: How the choice of bonds would affect the discount rate used for the liabilities
In a market‐consistent valuation, the discount rate would usually be the risk‐free rate obtainable
on government bonds regardless of the choice of assets.                                          [½]
However, an illiquidity premium can sometimes be included in the discount rate used for the
liabilities to take credit for the illiquidity premium in the yield on the assets held.     [½]
A higher discount rate will lead to a lower value of the liabilities, …                                  [½]
… and so a better solvency position.                                                                     [½]
If the insurer has chosen to hold the government bonds, it will not be able to include an illiquidity
premium in its discount rate.                                                                      [½]
If the insurer has chosen to hold either of the corporate bonds, then it may be able to include an
illiquidity premium, …                                                                           [½]
… if this is permitted by regulation / legislation.                                                      [½]
It is only generally appropriate to include an illiquidity premium for long‐term, predictable
liabilities …                                                                                            [½]
… which will be the case here if the insurer has a large enough portfolio to remove random
fluctuations from the experience.                                                                        [½]
The corporate bonds have similar yields overall, however, they are made up quite differently: [½]
         the multinational company’s bonds have a high risk of default, but are very marketable /
liquid, …                                                                              [½]
… so the majority of the yield margin (above the government bond yield) will reflect the
default risk                                                                            [½]
         the small private company’s bonds have a lower risk of default, but are very unmarketable
/ illiquid, …                                                                         [½]
… so the majority of the yield margin (above the government bond yield) will reflect the
illiquidity risk.                                                                       [½]
Therefore, if the insurer held the multinational company’s bonds, then the scope for including an
illiquidity premium would be small, …                                                           [½]
… whereas if the insurer held the small private company’s bonds, then a significant allowance may
be made in the discount rate for illiquidity.                                                  [½]
[Maximum 4]

22.5.label: Question 22.5
22.5.scenario:
A health insurance company has a large and well‐established portfolio of single premium
   without‐profits pre‐funded and immediate needs long‐term care contracts. Both contracts pay
pre‐specified annual amounts when the insured requires care. No benefits are paid on
withdrawal for the immediate needs contract, or death.

(i).question:
Describe how this insurance company could use a market‐consistent approach to value its
liabilities.

Market‐consistent approach to value the: To determine the liabilities, future unknown parameter values and cashflows are set so as to be
consistent with market values, where a corresponding market exists.                            [½]
The following market‐consistent paramete:          mortality for the pre‐funded and immediate needs long‐term care annuities                              [½]
         long‐term care inception rates for the pre‐funded long‐term care contract                              [½]
         withdrawal rates for the pre‐funded long‐term care contract                                            [½]
         expenses and inflation for both contracts.                                                             [½]
For example, a market‐consistent estimate for the expense inflation parameter could be obtained
by considering the difference between the yield on fixed‐interest bonds and the real yield on
index‐linked bonds.                                                                           [½]
However, it may be difficult to obtain a ‘market‐consistent’ assumption for some elements of the
basis, such as mortality, morbidity or expenses, for which there is not a sufficiently deep and
liquid market in which to trade or hedge such risks.                                            [1]
The starting point for such assumptions would be a best estimate basis.                                          [½]
It is then likely that a risk margin would be included in respect of these assumptions, due to the
inherent uncertainty.                                                                             [½]
This risk margin would reflect the compensation required by the ‘market’ in return for taking on
those uncertain aspects of the liability cashflows …                                            [½]
… for example, the longevity risk for the annuities and the additional morbidity risk for the pre‐
funded contract.                                                        [½ for any suitable example]
The risk margin could be taken by adding a margin to each such assumption …                             [½]
… for example, the mortality assumption for the annuities could be lower than the best estimate.
[½ for any suitable example]
Alternatively, the risk margin could be:       project the required capital at each future time period (ie the amount required in excess of
      multiply the projected capital amounts by the cost of capital                                    [½]
      discount using market‐consistent discount rates to give the overall risk margin.                 [½]
The projected cashflows are discounted at a rate based on risk‐free rates irrespective of the type
‘Risk‐free’ rates may be determined based on government bond yields or on swap rates.                    [½]
Corporate bonds typically have a higher yield than risk‐free (eg government) bonds, where this
reflects both the greater default risk and the relative illiquidity of such assets.            [1]
It may be possible in some jurisdictions to take credit for the illiquidity premium and thereby
discount liabilities at a higher yield than the risk‐free rate.                                         [½]
Whatever the risk‐free rates are based on, an appropriate deduction should be made for any

(ii).question:
Explain whether it would be appropriate to take credit for the illiquidity premium in the
valuation of the liabilities.

Appropriateness of taking credit for the: The following three conditions must hold for the insurance company to take credit for the
         it must hold corporate bonds (or similar assets) with a higher yield than the risk‐free
         its liabilities must be long‐term and predictable                                             [½]
         matching assets must be held to maturity.                                                     [½]
If these conditions hold then the insurer is not exposed to the risk of changing spreads on its
assets (although is still exposed to default risk), ...                                                 [½]
... and so it may be appropriate to increase the risk‐free discount rate to allow for the illiquidity
Whether an illiquidity premium can be allowed for will depend on the purpose of the valuation
and the rules that apply in the country.                                                      [½]
Corporate bonds: Corporate bonds are more risky as the insurance company is still exposed to the risk of default.
The company may consider this risk to be too high given its risk appetite.                       [½]
The illiquidity premium cannot be allowed for if the company chooses to invest in safer risk‐free
Long‐term predictable liabilities: The pre‐funded immediate needs contracts could be long term, ...                                                  [½]
... however the immediate needs contracts are likely to be short to medium term.                                  [½]
The insurance company has a large portfolio, which will reduce the risk of random fluctuations. [½]
The cashflows relating to the annuities depend on the future mortality of the policyholders, which
should be reasonably predictable for a large portfolio.                                        [½]
However, the pre‐funded long‐term care c:          long‐term care inception rates, which are unlikely to be very predictable                               [½]
         withdrawals, in particular early in the contract (before a claim has incepted), which are
likely to depend on the political and economic environment.                               [½]
If, for example, the insurance company under‐estimated the number of withdrawals, it would
need to sell bonds that it had intended to hold until their maturity. This would expose the
company to any change in spreads on the bonds, ie the company would be exposed to the risk
Therefore, an allowance for the illiquidity premium may be appropriate for the immediate needs
long‐term care contracts if they were deemed to be long enough term, ...                     [½]
... but it is unlikely to be appropriate for the pre‐funded long‐term care contracts due to
Matching assets held until maturity: If the insurance company cannot find assets that match the term of the liabilities then it will need
to buy and sell assets in the future at unknown prices. So an allowance for the illiquidity premium
For example, the company may not be able to find suitable asserts with a long enough term.                        [½]
Similarly, if the company does not wish to be constrained to hold its assets to maturity it will be
exposed to the future uncertainty of bond prices, and so an allowance for the illiquidity premium

(iii).question:
Explain whether the regulator would consider the market‐consistent value of the liabilities
to be adequate for the calculation of statutory solvency.                               [4]

A recent investigation has revealed that withdrawal rates on the pre‐funded long‐term care
portfolio have risen significantly over the last twelve months, and are expected to continue at this
new level in future.

Solution: Adequacy of market‐consistent value of liabilities for statutory solvency
It is usual for regulators to require that a health insurance company maintain (at least) a specified
level of solvency capital.                                                                         [½]
This solvency capital can be seen as providing an additional level of protection to policyholders.
In considering the adequacy of the reserves that have been set up, it is important to do this within
the context of the solvency capital requirements.                                                [½]
Market risks: The market‐consistent valuation is performed using a discount rate derived from the return on
risk‐free assets regardless of the actual assets held.                                        [½]
There is a risk that the actual investment return achieved is less than the assumed risk‐free rate.
This risk can become considerable if the company decides to invest in risky assets such as
The discount rate could be adjusted for the illiquidity premium available on corporate bonds, but
there is a risk that these bonds show higher default rates than expected.                      [½]
So the regulator will want the insurance company to hold solvency capital in addition to the
market‐consistent value of the liabilities in order to protect policyholders from these market risks.
Non‐market risks: The market‐consistent valuation is performed using best estimate assumptions for mortality,
So there is a 50% chance that the actual experience is worse than that expected in the market‐
The company will hold some form of risk margin against these risks as part of its market‐
However, this risk margin is unlikely to be sufficient and so the regulator will also want the
insurance company to hold solvency capital against these risks too.                                  [½]
For example, if the risk margin is calculated using the cost of capital method then it will be the
discounted value of the cost of capital, rather than the full value of the capital required.       [½]

(iv).question:
Discuss any implications this may have for the suitability of the current valuation
approach, outlining any further investigations the insurance company might wish to carry
out for the purpose of ensuring that the valuation remains suitable in the future.

Implications of increased withdrawal rat: The mortality, morbidity, persistency and expense assumptions should be a best estimate of
future experience.                                                                                   [½]
Increased withdrawal rates will mean that more surrenders values are paid than expected. This
would mean that the current valuation is an underestimate if surrender values are more generous
than the current valuation.                                                                   [1]
Increased withdrawals will cause a fall in the volume of business in force.                                         [½]
All else being equal, this will cause a rise in future per‐policy expense levels, as overheads will be
covered by fewer in‐force policies.                                                                  [½]
So the current per policy expense assumption will be lower than a best estimate of future
experience, and so the expenses assumed should be increased.                                                        [½]
If withdrawal rates are not uniform over policies of different expired durations, the mix of the in‐
force business by duration will be altered and therefore the current valuation basis may no longer
be appropriate.                                                                                   [½]
The insurance company would need to investigate withdrawal rates by policy duration in order to
identify the extent of this problem.                                                         [½]
One of the greatest threats to the suitability of the valuation basis is from possible selective
withdrawals.                                                                                                        [½]
If withdrawals are predominantly occurring amongst healthy, low‐risk policyholders, the
morbidity experience of the remaining policyholders will worsen in aggregate.                                       [½]
The greater the volume of withdrawals, the more pronounced this could be.                                           [½]
The current valuation could then significantly understate the best estimate as a result.                            [½]
The insurer must ensure it monitors its aggregate mortality and morbidity experience for the
ongoing business on a regular basis, ...                                                     [½]
... so that it can quickly identify if its mortality and morbidity experience is deteriorating as a
consequence of these withdrawals (although this may be hard in the short term).                                     [½]
Withdrawal rates may also not be uniform by general mix of business. For example, policies
covering people from certain geographical areas or socio‐economic classes may be subsidising
other policies, so a non‐uniform withdrawal experience could change the mix of business in force
and so again impact on the suitability of the current assumption.                              [1]
Again, an investigation into withdrawal rates by these factors would be needed to identify the
extent of this.                                                                                [½]
[Maximum 5]

22.6.label: Question 22.6
22.6.scenario: A health and care insurer sells stand‐alone critical illness business.
22.6.question: Discuss the relative merits of using a passive valuation approach.

Solution: The expenses of the critical illness insurances are likely to be different from those of the
long‐term care insurances, for a number of reasons:
         a different level and/or type of underwriting on the two types of policy would lead to
different initial underwriting expenses
         a different level and/or type of claims management on the two types of policy would lead
to different claims handling expenses
         the simpler nature of the contract may reduce ongoing expenses
There will be high parameter risk, because, although data may be credible, they have little
relevance. The reasons include different:
          health of the population due to different environment, lifestyles, behaviour etc
The sickness experience is the most important element in the basis and so is a significant risk. On
the other hand, in an established market there is likely to be good data on which to base
assumptions.
Also, population sickness has typically been reasonably stable, or at least subject to steady trends,
in developed countries. So there should be low model and parameter risk. Even so, the
possibility of a new disease represents a risk that is difficult to quantify.
The risk to a particular insurance company will also depend on the portfolio size. The bigger the
portfolio, the bigger the potential loss. On the other hand, a larger portfolio should reduce
random fluctuations risk.
Usually, no. For CI policies where the probability of a claim is relatively low, the reserves held for
such policies will also be low. When reserves are low, investment income is relatively
unimportant because the funds to invest are small.
However, it is worth noting that for the most comprehensive CI policies (eg those in South Africa),
which offer benefits payable on a wide range of conditions (making an eventual claim on the
policy a likely outcome), reserves will be more significant and investment returns more important.
Even where policy conditions (and hence claims) are more restricted, reserves can be quite large
for policies with long, guaranteed terms.
For insurers paying claims in foreign currencies (which is more likely for PMI business than
any other products in the Subject SP1 syllabus), the insurer will be exposed to exchange
rate movements and may need to invest in assets in the relevant foreign currencies.
(a)        Too much new business suggests over‐stretched resources. Buying in extra capacity may
be difficult, expensive and inefficient (eg untrained staff, or existing staff working
overtime). This can lead to delays or mistakes (eg in underwriting), which can generate
bad public relations and/or risks (such as marketing risk).
(b)        Too little new business suggests excess capacity: costs may not be covered by premiums /
charges and profit per policy will fall. This reflects the fact that some costs are fixed, at
least in the short term, so they will be incurred regardless of business volume.
The parameters for future expenses, mentioned in Section 4, will be based on a model of
future new business. If actual new business differs significantly from expected levels of
future new business, then the insurer’s expense assumptions may become invalid.
These parameters for future expenses relate not only to future new business but also to in‐force
business.
There are some expenses involved in running the business that are neither directly related to
volumes, nor obviously associated with either new business or in‐force business, eg the Chief
Executive’s salary. The more business that comes onto the books, the more policies there are
over which the insurer can spread such expenses, and so the smaller the per‐policy expense
assumption can be.
As in any other industry, a significant fall in volumes of new business will damage an insurer’s
profitability, its level of efficiency, and hence competitiveness, and perhaps even its long‐term
viability.
It should be noted that, for short-term health insurance business, risk management and
monitoring may be more focussed on the overall volume and mix of in-force business rather
than specifically on new business. The risks relating to the overall volume and mix of
business are a combination of new business and persistency risks.
Compared to active valuation approaches,: +         tend to be more straightforward to implement                                                              [½]
+         tend to involve less subjectivity, …                                                                      [½]
… eg a net premium valuation is relatively insensitive to the choice of valuation basis                   [½]
+         tend to result in relatively stable emergence of accounting profit                               [½]
+         may be less likely to be subject to procyclicality and systemic risk:                            [½]
          a passive valuation of assets (such as historic book value) would be little affected
by volatile asset prices                                                           [½]
          an active approach (such as market value) may result in the need to sell risky
assets after a fall in prices, which could lead to further price falls               [½]
–         are at risk of becoming out of date, …                                                           [½]
… and hence management might fail to take appropriate actions in time; …                         [½]
… in particular, they might fail to take account of important trends, …                          [½]
… such as increasing claim incidence rates due to earlier diagnoses of certain
illnesses …                                                                                      [½]
… or increasing inflation of regular expenses and claims expenses                                [½]
–         may provide a false sense of security when the reality is that market conditions have
changed significantly                                                                            [½]
–         tend to be less informative in terms of understanding the impact of market conditions on
the ability of the company to meet its obligations ...                                 [½]
… particularly in relation to financial guarantees and options …                                 [½]
… although it is unlikely that a CI insurance policy would include any financial options and
guarantees, ...                                                                            [½]
… and it is unlikely to be exposed to the equity market …                                        [½]
… although it may be exposed to price volatility if it holds corporate bonds.              [½]
[Maximum 6]

Syllabus objective: 3.1        Assess how the following can be a source of risk to a health and care insurance
0 Introduction: This chapter and the following two chapters look at the macro risks with which a health and
care insurance company might be faced.
This chapter and the next cover the possible sources of these risks. The third of the three
chapters on risk discusses the problems that those risks can present to an insurer.
‘Micro’ (ie contract‐specific) risks were discussed earlier in the course. However these risks might
equally well be thought of as specific examples of some of the ‘macro’ risks covered in this
chapter. For example, uncertainty over future investment returns is a general risk for a health
insurer and so is included here. However, it has already been seen that its importance as a risk
varies depending on the type of contract involved.
This chapter concentrates in more detail on how and why an insurer’s assumptions about the
future may turn out to be incorrect. Indeed, the general theme of much of this chapter and the
next considers that:
1.        Actuaries are constantly involved in investigations that require them to make assumptions
about a wide range of future events. These investigations form the basis of the insurer’s
strategic decision‐making (for example for pricing, product design, investment strategy
and reinsurance strategy).
2.        For various reasons reality will turn out differently from expected (ie assumed). The
extent to which these deviations are adverse constitutes the insurer’s risk. Hence each
assumption represents a source of risk.
Other risks (that don’t relate to experience being different from expected) will also be considered,
for example the effects of competition and the management of the insurance company.
The identification of risks is an essential part of a health and care insurance company’s control
cycle. Any business will wish to identify threats to its financial wellbeing and profit stream.
Having identified the risks, the insurance company can put in place mechanisms to monitor them.
It can also do a number of things to limit and control the risks that it faces. These chapters
concentrate primarily on the sources of risk rather than the control and monitoring, which will be
covered later.
1.1 Policy data: A health and care insurance company will maintain a database of the policies it has on its books.
For each policy, this will include items such as age at entry, date of entry, policy term, date of
diagnosis (where relevant) and many others, dependent on policy type.
Having high quality policy data is vital for the good management of an insurance company.
One important use of policy data is to carry out a regular valuation of the liabilities, usually,
but not exclusively, for insurance supervisory purposes. These data will be maintained by
the insurance company and the actuary carrying out a valuation will usually not have a
direct control over them. There is, therefore, a risk that the company will not maintain the
adequate, accurate and complete records required by the actuary and hence the result of
the valuation will not itself be accurate.
The seriousness of this risk depends on the size and nature of the error. In the extreme, an
insurance company might declare itself to be solvent when it was insolvent, or vice‐versa.
However, even less serious errors could lead to incorrect decisions, for example through
underestimating the capital available to fund new business.
A similar point relates to policy data required for any internal investigations carried out by
the actuary to give appropriate advice to the company.
If the data are so inaccurate as to lead the actuary to give incorrect advice then this is potentially
very serious, both for the insurance company and the actuary. Therefore the actuary would
normally carry out some checks on the quality of the data. It is imperative that the actuary is at
least aware of any data problems.
For some investigations, model points representing all or part of the business may be used.
There will be a risk that these model points do not adequately represent the underlying
policy data.
So there are two aspects to data risk: 1.         The data of the insurance company might be inaccurate, inadequate or incomplete, so
that any model of the business in force that has relied on these data will be similarly
affected. This is a data risk.
2.         Where model points have been used to represent the business in force, there is a risk that
these will not represent the characteristics of the actual portfolio well enough to produce
valid results, leading to incorrect business decisions being made. This is a model (or
modelling) risk: it is the risk that results from the model being an over‐simplification of
the actual situation in terms of the portfolio of policies currently in force, and as a
1.2 Other data: Other data useful to actuarial investigations might include information on asset holdings
and external data. The latter may be needed to determine morbidity and mortality rates.
External data sources include insurance industry data, national statistics and data from overseas
It may be that the data internal to the company will not be adequate to provide reliable
indicators of future experience, because of inaccuracy and/or insufficient volume. Even
where adequate, data may prove to be inappropriate because of differences between the
population on which it was based and the population for which it will be used. This is
especially true where the actuary uses population data, overseas data or even insured data
where policy conditions and underwriting / claims procedures may differ significantly from
 there may not be enough data f: Even if the data are of good quality, they may not be suitable as a basis for future assumptions
(unless they are significantly modified), because they may be based on an inappropriate class of
Question: A health insurer has written large volumes of long‐term care insurance over many years. It has
performed a careful analysis of the average expense per policy of this business. It is about to start
writing critical illness insurance for the first time, and it plans to use the per‐policy expenses from
its long‐term care insurance as its expense assumption for the critical illness insurance.
Insurers in a number of countries developing long‐term care contracts have made use of
experience from the United States, where the market is more established, from which more
precise parameter estimates can be calculated because of the greater volume of data available.
Explain whether this practice is likely to lead to large or small parameter risk in the countries
concerned.
Discuss the extent to which sickness experience represents a risk on IP insurance for an insurance
company operating in an established insurance market in a country with a developed economy.
For CI products, there is also (usually) a significant time gap between premium receipt and claim
settlement. So is investment performance also of great significance for this product?
Why don’t insurers just charge a fixed fee for contracts, irrespective of their size, to get round the
problem of mix by size of contract?
Suggest two key factors related to distribution channel that may cause persistency experience to
vary between channels.

The expenses incurred in the past might:           inflation that has occurred since the time period that has been analysed and inflation that
is expected in the future (depending on how long premium rates will remain unchanged)
          changes in the insurer’s processes and/or systems, eg greater efficiency.
Alternatively, any external data used may be inadequate or unreliable, and even where it is
reliable may prove to be inappropriate for the same reason as above.
This is because of differences between the population on which it was based and the population
for which it will be used.
2.1 Claim rates: In most of the investigations carried out by an actuary, assumptions will need to be made
regarding future mortality, incidence of critical illness and incidence and duration of
sickness, as appropriate. There are three risks associated with these assumptions:

The main risk is that mortality or morbidity turns out to be more adverse than assumed in the
models used for decision‐making. These three types of risk help illustrate the various ways in
which the risk can arise. This in turn can help an insurer to devise suitable means of controlling
the risk. In other words, understanding where the risk has come from is half the battle.
As the Core Reading says, the likelihood of the assumptions for the future being right depends on
the quality and relevance of the data available. In other words, poor or irrelevant data will
increase the parameter risk:
          for a well‐established type of contract, with reasonably stable and predictable experience,
and lots of relevant data on which to base assumptions, the risk would be low
          for a contract type that is completely new to the market, where the actuary would have
to rely on a mixture of intelligent guesswork and heavy adaptation of what little relevant
data exist, the risk would be high.
Furthermore, there will always be an expanding funnel of doubt, ie the uncertainty in a
parameter’s value increases the further into the future the projection goes, however good the
experience data may be. For example, the possibility of new diseases or sudden advances in
medical treatment can never be ruled out, but these are extremely difficult to predict.
 policy wordings causing claim:           levels of underwriting and claims management between countries.
So the data would need adjusting before they could be used, to allow for the differences. These
adjustments might be at best intuitive guesswork, and so there is a high risk of the parameter
Note: the expression ‘claim rates’ here represents claim frequency. For PMI business,
insurers may use the term ‘claim rate’ to represent total claims incurred divided by the
exposed to risk measure, ie a combination of frequency and amount.
2.2 Identifying the type of risk: There are no dogmatic rights or wrongs about how much of the risk associated with a particular
variable is due to model, parameter or random fluctuations risks.
Model and parameter risk: Parameter risk is essentially the risk that the parameters used in the underlying model of the
future are ‘wrong’, however, sometimes the distinction between model and parameter risk is
rather blurred. For example, mortality might be rising over time, in which case the future
mortality parameters would be wrong, but the underlying reason for this may be that the model
has failed to allow for future mortality changes. So, the fact that the mortality parameters are
wrong might be due to model risk, rather than parameter risk.
Random fluctuations risk: Heterogeneity might exist as a result of the model being overly simplistic (so is arguably actually
model risk). For example, the model might only assume that claim rates vary by age (and so use
an age‐dependent claim rate assumption), whereas in fact claim rates are also likely to vary by
gender, occupation, smoker status etc. This heterogeneity may disguise a great deal of variation
in the population that could be modelled better by splitting it down further, eg by gender and
The law of large numbers indicates that unpredictable fluctuations are larger in small samples, but
even in large samples there will be some random fluctuation, and so there will always be random
2.3 Claim amounts: Under private medical insurance, a further element of risk lies in the estimation of future
average claim amounts for each of the various procedures covered by the policy. In most
markets, the requirement to predict is seldom for more than a year ahead. Nonetheless, the
fact that the insurer is generally ‘guaranteeing’ to cover whatever a third party (hospital or
other health service provider) may charge, introduces short-term uncertainty.
This additional uncertainty for private medical insurance can be significant. For example,
advances in treatment meant that the typical rehabilitation period following hip replacement
surgery reduced from a few weeks to a few days within a matter of months. The difference in
cost between a few days and a few weeks in‐patient care amounts to thousands of pounds per
The average cost of claims will vary as inflation increases costs year on year and as the mix
of the insured members changes (eg by age, health status, location). Additionally, however,
          different medical procedures are performed to treat the same conditions (as medical
          hospital capacity changes the likely stay as in-patient for a given procedure
          pricing agreements are negotiated and re-negotiated with providers.
2.4 Earlier screening / diagnosis: A very specific risk arising in health insurance is that brought about by advances in medical
science. It was noted above that the cost of treatment for particular illnesses might rise
because new and more expensive treatments and equipment are now in use. A further risk,
of particular relevance to critical illness insurance, is that of earlier diagnosis.
Typically, a critical illness policy will pay the sum insured when the policyholder is
unequivocally diagnosed as suffering from a particular disease. If medical technology
advances in the diagnostic area, insurers may find that they are facing claims significantly
earlier than was expected on the basis of the data on which premiums were calculated.
Indeed, the earlier diagnosis may prompt some claims that would not otherwise have been
payable (because the policyholder might have died prior to diagnosis, or lapsed, or a fixed
term policy might have expired).
The encouragement by medical professionals for members of the public to get themselves
screened or the launch of national screening campaigns for particular illnesses, such as
breast cancer, will accelerate this issue. PMI insurers are also at risk of increased claims
incidence due to higher prevalence of screening programmes. However, they are not
necessarily at risk of increased cost, as illnesses may be identified earlier and thus require
less treatment.
The overall impact on claim costs depends on the additional cost of screening and how
many insured members get screened relative to how many members are subsequently
diagnosed with conditions that require further treatment.
Similarly, the increasing availability of many (formerly complex) treatments, such as
coronary by-pass grafting, is another risk area for CI insurance providers and PMI writers.
The main problem here is that for PMI, and for CI benefits based on surgical procedures
(eg coronary by‐pass grafting), greater availability of treatment means that more of these
treatments are being claimed for under the policy, leading to a direct increase in claims.
A secondary problem, at least for the insurer, is that these treatments prolong the lives of the
sufferers, who will be more likely to need treatment later in life following the treatment. This
may be a problem for PMI and stand‐alone CI.
3.1 Future investment: In most of the investigations carried out by actuaries, assumptions, either on a
deterministic or stochastic basis, will need to be made as to the rate of return, allowing for
both income and capital appreciation, on amounts to be invested in the future. Both model
and parameter risks can therefore arise.
 future premiums on new busines: In the past, actuaries typically made deterministic assumptions about future investment returns
and the effect of possible variations was explored by sensitivity testing. Stochastic modelling is
now a commonly‐used alternative, under which the investment returns on asset classes in any
given period are modelled as the outcomes of random variables with specified probability
distributions. Chapter 28 contains an example of such a model.
Model risks might arise if there are errors in the model. In the context of a stochastic investment
model, an example of this might be an inappropriate probability distribution for the random error
term, eg normal when it should have been log‐normal. Parameter risks would exist if the
parameters underlying these distributions were inappropriate.
3.2 Existing assets: Some investigations will require assumptions, again either on a deterministic or stochastic
basis, as to the future income and capital appreciation in respect of all, or parts of, the
existing assets of the company. Again, there can be model and parameter risks.
This would be necessary when the insurer is performing any projection that involves its existing
Investigations involving a comparison of the value of the liabilities with the value of the
assets will be subject also to a capital values risk in respect of the existing assets being
Investigations of this sort tend to be of a ‘snapshot’ nature, at one point in time. The risk is that
an insurance company that appears solvent on one day could become insolvent the following day
after a change in asset values.
3.3 Asset management: The passage of responsibility for the process or management of investments to third parties
However, it is hoped that the expertise that the third party would bring would make that extra
risk worthwhile. In any case, any sensible insurer would choose the investment company
3.4 Significance of investment perform: Investment performance may be of lesser significance when considering private medical
insurance due to its short tail from premium receipt to claim settlement (typically no longer
For some long-term insurances, particularly pre-funded long-term care, the investment
assumptions and hence risks may be major considerations.
4 Expenses and inflation: In most investigations carried out by actuaries, assumptions will need to be made as to the
future expenses to be incurred by the company. These will allow, implicitly or explicitly,
either on a deterministic or stochastic basis, for inflation. There is therefore a parameter
risk. In addition, there will be a model risk if stochastic assumptions are being used.
When a stochastic approach to investment returns is used, it is normal to also model inflation
stochastically. In fact, the model may be ‘driven’ by a stochastically generated inflation rate, with
investment returns linked in some way to the projected inflation. The inflation of a health and
care insurer’s expenses may not be the same as general price inflation in the economy, but can be
linked to it in the model.
The company will include amounts designed to cover its expenses, either explicitly or
implicitly, in the premiums that it charges to policyholders. These charges may be
augmented by others made (again either explicitly or implicitly) against the funds built up by
contracts.
On conventional contracts, the charges for expenses may be invisible to the policyholder, as they
are loaded within the quoted premium rate. Sometimes, a fixed policy fee to cover some part of
the expenses will be quoted explicitly.
In contrast, for unit‐linked contracts, charges are explicit. They may be deducted from premiums
(eg by allocating less than 100% of the premiums to units) or from the fund (eg through a regular
policy fee paid for by unit cancellation).
In either case, it is not likely to be possible to design the charges so that they correspond exactly
to the expenses of administering the product, and change in line with any changes in expenses.
There is, therefore, a risk that the charges accruing to the company in a year will not cover
the actual expenses of the company in that year.
Examples of such risks are:          when heavy up‐front expenses are recouped gradually by charges made over the term of
the policy, the insurer is vulnerable to the policy being withdrawn
         on policies of long duration, where higher‐than‐expected inflation may mean that the
original expense loadings prove to be inadequate
         the influence of business volumes on the insurer’s ability to cover its fixed expenses.
Risks become wider when third parties are involved and the ultimate expense costs are
difficult to estimate. For example, income protection insurers and PMI insurers may use
third parties to manage their claims. This additional outgo might be deemed part of the
overall claim amount (and loaded as such) especially if costs vary directly with the amount
5.1 Introduction: In most investigations carried out by actuaries, assumptions will need to be made as to the
future persistency of the contracts issued by the company (based on lapse rates for long-
term business and renewal rates for short-term business). There is therefore a parameter
risk and there may also be a model risk.
For example, the model might link persistency to stochastically generated investment returns
(perhaps as a proxy for general economic conditions), however, such a link might not reflect
reality. This would be an example of model risk.
Even if the structure of the model is correct, the parameters might be wrong.
Random fluctuations risk could also affect persistency, especially within small portfolios.
5.2 Selective withdrawals: The parameters considered in Section 2 will be based on a model of the selective effect of
persistency. Departures from the latter may invalidate the former.
Selective withdrawals on health and care insurance contracts are a risk because they leave behind
a ‘substandard’ group of policyholders. Insurers know this and so can allow for the expected
effect in pricing. However, the risk of more selective withdrawals than expected still exists and
this could lead to worse claims experience than had been assumed.
5.3 Persistency and expenses: The parameters for future expenses, mentioned in Section 4, will be based on a model of
future persistency. Departures from the latter may invalidate the former.
Higher than expected withdrawals mean that fixed costs have to be spread over fewer policies.
This may produce higher per‐policy expenses than the insurer had allowed for in its pricing.
Also, such departures may be a cause of the mismatch, mentioned in Section 4, between
the charges accruing to the company and its actual expenses.
If an insurer is recouping initial expenses gradually over the term of a contract then there is a
mismatch in the timing of income (charges) and outgo (expenses). The amount of charges to
recoup the initial expenses will have been set, when the contract was priced, on the basis of
assumed rates of future withdrawal. Higher than expected withdrawals would then make the
future income from these charges inadequate to repay the initial expenses.
Persistency rates will be closely monitored under private medical insurance where often
initial costs and maybe commissions are amortised over an assumed average number of
PMI is a short‐term contract, where the insurer relies on a certain level of persistency (renewals)
in order to recoup the high initial acquisition costs. If renewals turn out to be lower than
expected, there is a high chance that the insurer will be unable to recoup all the costs.
5.4 Lapse and re‐entry: The risks of lapse and re-entry are particularly acute in any market where increasing
statistical information and/or aggressive competition is driving the new business price
down. This has been a feature of many critical illness insurance markets, fed, in part, by an
Business mix may be adversely affected by selective or biased lapses or non-renewals.
Selective lapses often occur due to information asymmetries between the insurer and the
policyholder, and they usually directly affect the overall claims experience of the insurer. In
particular, it tends to be policyholders who know that they are lower risk deliberately lapsing their
polices, leaving the insurer with, on average, worse risks.
Biased lapses refers to lapses that have certain features, but which aren’t necessarily due to a
selective action by policyholders. For example, in a recession, there might be a tendency for small
policies to lapse (as policyholders with the smallest sums insured may be the ones who are now
no longer able to afford their policies); this would leave the insurer with larger policies on
average. Biased lapses will affect the mix of the remaining business, which may then affect future
In summary, the key persistency risks ar:          the risk that the asset share is negative at the time of withdrawal, which will be the case
         the risk to the claims experience due to the selective effect of withdrawals
         the risk of increasing the per‐policy fixed expenses due to the loss of business volume
6.1 The mix by nature and size of cont: A significant unintended change in the mix of new business by nature or by size of contract
could lead to a significant change in the risk profile or capital needs of the company that
were not within the resources available to it.
 premium frequency (single vs: If the risks involved in the business, or the capital required to write it, vary by any of these
aspects, then the overall risks and capital needs of the insurer will change if the mix changes.
If contracts are smaller on average than had been assumed, the income from charges and
premium loadings will be reduced, and this is unlikely to be matched entirely by a reduction in
expenses. This will occur especially on conventional products, where most of the charges are
likely to be related to the premium size, while a larger proportion of the expenses may be per
policy, and hence independent of size.
However, one significant problem is that a fixed fee can make smaller policies seem too expensive
and uncompetitive. Even though small contracts will probably be less profitable than large ones,
insurance companies are still interested in writing smaller contracts in order to get some
contribution to cover fixed expenses (as long as all the variable costs are at least met).
A possible solution is to charge a fixed fee that covers part of the expenses, while still accepting
some cross‐subsidy from large contracts to small ones.
A significant unintended change in the mix may be another cause of the mismatch,
mentioned in Section 4, between the charges accruing to the company and its actual
A mismatch between charges and expenses might result from a change in the mix of contracts by
nature and size. For example, an insurance company may have to accept a relatively low
contribution to total overhead expenses from a particular type of contract. This could arise if that
contract were subject to greater competition, keeping premium rates or charges low.
If the mix of business were to change so that more of this contract were sold and less of other
contracts carrying higher overhead loadings, the insurance company might not receive a sufficient
6.2 The mix by source: The parameters adopted for the assumptions for claim inception, mortality and recovery
rates, average claim amounts and expenses will be based, where appropriate, on an
expected mix of new business by source. A change in the mix by source may invalidate
‘Source’ here means distribution channel. As discussed in an earlier chapter, the different
populations reached by different distribution channels can exhibit different experience for
mortality and other claim parameters, as well as persistency.
 the level of financial sophist:          the degree of pressure of the sale, which may be affected by who initiated the sale
Expenses of distribution also vary by channel. For example, the amounts of the salespeople’s
A health insurance company may wish to charge the same price for a product through all its
distribution channels, despite the different expected experience. It would therefore have to use
some sort of weighted average of the experience, dependent on the expected mix by source. If
the mix turned out differently then the insurance company might make or lose money, but the
Conversely, if a health insurance company prices differently for the different distribution
channels, and can achieve the same level of profitability in the different channels, then it is not
exposed to this risk of changes in the mix by source.
7 The volume of new business: A health and care insurance company may experience problems as a result of writing too much or
7.1 High volumes of business: An insurance company will need to ensure that the capital and administrative requirements
of writing new business are within the resources available to it. An inappropriately high
volume of new business could, therefore, lead to the available resources becoming
An insurer’s capital is the excess of its assets over its supervisory liabilities and any required
solvency capital. Writing new business uses up this capital, largely because of prudent statutory
reserving and/or solvency capital requirements and high initial expenses. If the insurer writes too
much business, then it might have to impose limits on further new business.
In the extreme, it could make itself insolvent in the regulators’ eyes.
7.2 Low volumes of business: On the other hand, inappropriately low volumes of a newly introduced product line may
pose a risk of not recovering the fixed development costs that will already have been
Chapter 23 Summary: In most investigations, actuaries have to make assumptions about the future. Because the
future cannot be predicted with certainty, there are risks attached to making these
These risks may often be usefully classi:           model risk – the risk that the underlying model is not adequate
          parameter risk – the risk that the parameters assumed for the underlying model are
          random fluctuations risk – the risk of unpredictable fluctuations arising from sample
error – this is greater the smaller the sample.
Policy data: Inadequate, inaccurate or incomplete policy data could lead to incorrect results and
recommendations in actuarial investigations, including those performed for the supervisory
Where a model office is used, this may not represent the in‐force business accurately
Other data: Data used in the formulation of actuarial assumptions may be inadequate. Even where they
are adequate in themselves, they may not be applicable to the purpose for which the
Claims: The future parameters can never be predicted with certainty, because of, for example, new
diseases and advances in medical treatment. However, where there are good quality and
relevant data this reduces the risk of errors in parameter estimation.
For PMI, there is a risk in estimating future average claim amounts for the various
procedures covered by the policy. Average claim costs will rise with inflation, changing
treatments for conditions, changes in the length of stay in hospital and pricing agreement
Advances in medical science may mean that claims for health insurance may increase. Also,
screening is often encouraged, which may increase claims frequency.
Investment performance: Investment return assumptions may be needed for the existing assets or future investment
(and probably both). The importance of the assumption depends in part on the delay
between premium receipt and claim payment, and importantly on the size of the reserves.
Investment return might be modelled deterministically or stochastically. In either case,
there are model, parameter and random fluctuations risks.
In a stochastic approach the random fluctuations are modelled explicitly. In a deterministic
approach this risk can be explored through sensitivity testing.
There is also a ‘capital values risk’ in any investigation comparing existing assets with
Expenses: There is a risk of higher than expected expenses. The risk may be greater if third parties are
Contributions to expenses from premiums and charges may be significantly mismatched with
the actual expenses incurred, over time.
Higher than expected inflation of expenses leads to an expense risk. This can have model,
Persistency: The unpredictability of persistency makes it a risk. Early withdrawals usually represent a
particular risk of loss, because of the failure to recoup initial expenses.
If persistency experience is different from expected it may invalidate assumptions about the
effects of selective withdrawals on mortality, critical illness or sickness experience. Expense
assumptions may also be invalidated due to the effect of withdrawals on the volume of
The mix of business by nature and size o: The unpredictability of the mix of new business by nature and size of contract is a risk.
Unexpected variation in nature or size may change the risk profile of the insurer, which may
lead to an overstretching of its capital and other resources available to cover the risk.
Coverage of per‐policy expenses is particularly vulnerable to a reduction in the average size
The mix of business by source: Different distribution channels involve different sales methods and reach different
populations. As a result, the demographic and expense experience of the various channels is
likely to differ. Variation from the insurer’s assumed mix by source could therefore invalidate
the insurer’s demographic and expense assumptions.
Insurers may experience difficulties as:           too much business, so that its capital resources or administrative capacity are
          too little business, so that it fails to cover its fixed development costs / overhead
